title: Six lawful bases for processing
rule: Personal data may only be processed if at least one of six lawful bases applies: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.
details: Article 6(1) lists the six bases. The controller must identify and document the lawful basis before processing begins. The basis cannot be changed retrospectively.
exceptions: Special category data (Article 9) requires an additional condition beyond Article 6

title: Conditions for valid consent
rule: Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. It requires a clear affirmative action. Pre-ticked boxes do not constitute valid consent.
details: Article 7 and Recital 32. Consent must be as easy to withdraw as to give. Separate consent required for different processing purposes. Consent for children under 16 (or 13-16 depending on member state) requires parental authorization.
exceptions: Member states may lower the age of digital consent to 13 (Article 8(1))

title: Right of access (DSAR)
rule: Data subjects have the right to obtain confirmation of whether their data is being processed, and if so, access to that data and supplementary information.
details: Article 15. The controller must provide a copy of the data free of charge within one month. Can be extended by two months for complex requests. Must include: purposes, categories, recipients, retention period, rights, source, and automated decision-making info.
exceptions: May refuse manifestly unfounded or excessive requests (Article 12(5))
Must not adversely affect the rights of others

title: Right to erasure (right to be forgotten)
rule: Data subjects have the right to obtain erasure of their personal data without undue delay where one of several grounds applies.
details: Article 17. Grounds include: data no longer necessary, consent withdrawn, data unlawfully processed, legal obligation to erase. The controller must also inform other controllers processing the data.
exceptions: Does not apply where processing is necessary for: freedom of expression, legal obligation, public health, archiving in public interest, legal claims

title: Right to data portability
rule: Data subjects have the right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format and to transmit it to another controller.
details: Article 20. Applies where processing is based on consent or contract AND is carried out by automated means. The data subject may request direct transmission between controllers where technically feasible.
exceptions: Does not apply to processing based on legal obligation or public interest
Must not adversely affect the rights of others

title: 72-hour breach notification
rule: In the case of a personal data breach, the controller must notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware, unless the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to individuals.
details: Article 33. The notification must describe: nature of breach, categories and number of data subjects affected, contact details of DPO, likely consequences, and measures taken or proposed.
exceptions: Notification not required if breach is unlikely to result in a risk to rights and freedoms
If notification cannot be provided within 72 hours, reasons for delay must be given

title: Communication to data subjects
rule: When a breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, the controller must communicate the breach to the data subject without undue delay.
details: Article 34. Not required if: the controller has implemented appropriate technical measures (encryption), subsequent measures ensure high risk is no longer likely, or it would involve disproportionate effort (public communication instead).
exceptions: Not required if data was encrypted or otherwise unintelligible to unauthorized persons
Public communication may substitute individual notification if disproportionate effort

title: Data protection by design and by default
rule: The controller must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to integrate data protection into processing activities from the design stage, and ensure only necessary data is processed by default.
details: Article 25. By design: pseudonymisation, data minimisation built in from start. By default: only personal data necessary for each specific purpose is processed; not made accessible to an indefinite number of persons without intervention.

title: Data Protection Impact Assessment
rule: A DPIA is required when processing is likely to result in a high risk to individuals, particularly when using new technologies, systematic profiling, or large-scale processing of special categories.
details: Article 35. Must contain: systematic description of processing, assessment of necessity and proportionality, assessment of risks, and measures to address risks. Must consult the DPO. If high risk remains after mitigation, must consult the supervisory authority (Article 36).
exceptions: Not required for processing that is not likely to result in a high risk
Supervisory authorities publish lists of processing types requiring DPIA

title: Cookie consent (ePrivacy)
rule: Storing or accessing information on a user's device (cookies, local storage, fingerprinting) requires prior informed consent, except for strictly necessary cookies.
details: ePrivacy Directive 2002/58/EC Article 5(3), as interpreted by CJEU (Planet49 case). Consent must meet GDPR standards (freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous). Cookie walls that block access unless all cookies accepted are generally non-compliant.
exceptions: Strictly necessary cookies (session management, security, load balancing) are exempt
Analytics cookies require consent in most member states

title: Storage limitation principle
rule: Personal data must be kept in a form that permits identification for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which it is processed.
details: Article 5(1)(e). Controllers must establish retention periods for each category of personal data and document them. Data must be deleted or anonymized when no longer needed. Archiving in the public interest may justify longer retention with appropriate safeguards.
exceptions: Longer retention for archiving in the public interest, scientific/historical research, statistical purposes (Article 89(1))

title: International data transfers
rule: Personal data may only be transferred to a third country if the country provides an adequate level of protection, or appropriate safeguards are in place.
details: Chapter V. Adequacy decisions by the Commission (currently: Andorra, Argentina, Canada (commercial), Faroe Islands, Guernsey, Israel, Isle of Man, Japan, Jersey, New Zealand, South Korea, Switzerland, UK, Uruguay, USA (DPF)). Without adequacy: SCCs, BCRs, or derogations (Article 49).
exceptions: Derogations for specific situations: explicit consent, contract necessity, public interest, legal claims, vital interests (Article 49)

title: Administrative fines — upper tier
rule: Infringements of the basic principles for processing, data subjects' rights, and international transfer provisions can result in fines up to EUR 20 million or 4% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher.
details: Article 83(5). The upper tier applies to violations of: principles (Article 5), lawful basis (Article 6), consent (Articles 7-9), data subject rights (Articles 12-22), and transfers (Articles 44-49).
exceptions: Public authorities may be subject to different fine limits depending on member state law

title: Administrative fines — lower tier
rule: Infringements of controller/processor obligations, certification body obligations, and monitoring body obligations can result in fines up to EUR 10 million or 2% of total worldwide annual turnover.
details: Article 83(4). The lower tier covers: obligations of controller and processor (Articles 8, 11, 25-39, 42-43), certification body (Article 42-43), monitoring body (Article 41(4)).

title: Records of processing activities
rule: Controllers and processors with 250+ employees must maintain records of processing activities. Smaller organizations must maintain records if processing is not occasional, or involves special categories or criminal data.
details: Article 30. Controller records must include: name/contact, purposes, categories of data subjects and data, recipients, transfers, retention periods, security measures. Processor records: name/contact, categories of processing, transfers, security measures.
exceptions: Organizations under 250 employees exempt unless processing is not occasional, or involves risk, special categories, or criminal data

title: Data Protection Officer requirement
rule: A DPO must be designated where: (a) processing is carried out by a public authority, (b) core activities require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects on a large scale, or (c) core activities involve large-scale processing of special categories.
details: Articles 37-39. The DPO must be independent, have expert knowledge of data protection law, report to the highest management level, and be involved in all data protection matters. Contact details must be published and communicated to the supervisory authority.
exceptions: Courts acting in their judicial capacity are exempt
Member states may designate additional categories requiring a DPO

title: Data minimisation principle
rule: Personal data must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which it is processed.
details: Article 5(1)(c). Only collect what you need. Don't collect 'just in case'. Review data collected periodically to ensure it remains necessary. Applies to both the amount of data and the scope of processing.

title: Security of processing
rule: The controller and processor must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk.
details: Article 32. Measures include: pseudonymisation and encryption, ability to ensure confidentiality/integrity/availability/resilience, ability to restore data after incident, regular testing of security measures. Must consider state of the art, cost, nature/scope/context/purposes, and risk.

title: Information to be provided to data subjects
rule: When collecting personal data, the controller must inform the data subject of: identity, DPO contact, purposes, lawful basis, recipients, transfer intentions, retention period, rights, and whether provision is obligatory.
details: Articles 13-14. Must be provided at the time of data collection (direct) or within one month (indirect). Must be concise, transparent, intelligible, in clear and plain language. Layered privacy notices are recommended by regulators.
exceptions: Not required where the data subject already has the information
Indirect collection: exception if providing info would be impossible or involve disproportionate effort

title: Right to object to profiling and automated decisions
rule: Data subjects have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produce legal effects or similarly significant effects.
details: Article 22. Controller must implement suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights, at minimum the right to obtain human intervention, express their point of view, and contest the decision. Does not apply if necessary for contract, authorised by law, or based on explicit consent.
exceptions: Permitted if necessary for contract, authorised by EU/member state law, or based on explicit consent
Even where permitted, must provide human intervention on request
